subject: [PATCH v3 12/12] treewide: change inode->i_ino from unsigned long to u64

reviews.author: Damien Moal
reviews.raw_body:
On 3/5/26 00:32, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On 32-bit architectures, unsigned long is only 32 bits wide, which
> causes 64-bit inode numbers to be silently truncated. Several
> filesystems (NFS, XFS, BTRFS, etc.) can generate inode numbers that
> exceed 32 bits, and this truncation can lead to inode number collisions
> and other subtle bugs on 32-bit systems.
> 
> Change the type of inode->i_ino from unsigned long to u64 to ensure that
> inode numbers are always represented as 64-bit values regardless of
> architecture. Update all format specifiers treewide from %lu/%lx to
> %llu/%llx to match the new type, along with corresponding local variable
> types.
> 
> This is the bulk treewide conversion. Earlier patches in this series
> handled trace events separately to allow trace field reordering for
> better struct packing on 32-bit.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

For the zonefs bits:

Acked-by: Damien Le Moal <dlemoal@kernel.org>

-- 
Damien Le Moal
Western Digital Research


---

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary: Jan Kara reviewed the VFS, ext2, ext4, ocfs2, udf, isofs, jdb2, minix, fsnotify, and proc bits of the patch, finding them to be good and requesting a Reviewed-by tag.
reviews.raw_body:
I've reviewed the VFS, ext2, ext4, ocfs2, udf, isofs, jdb2, minix,
fsnotify, and proc bits and they look good to me. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>

								Honza

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch description mentions inode number truncation on 32-bit architectures, but did not verify if this issue actually occurs in practice.
reviews.raw_body:
Looks good:

Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>

2026-03-05.patch_summary: This patch changes the type of inode->i_ino from unsigned long to u64 to prevent silent truncation of 64-bit inode numbers on 32-bit architectures. This is necessary because several filesystems can generate inode numbers that exceed 32 bits, leading to collisions and bugs on 32-bit systems. The change updates format specifiers and local variable types throughout the kernel to match the new type. This patch is part of a larger series that converts various data structures to use u64 instead of unsigned long.
